threats.title: Delegation Logic Error Wipes Out All Delegated Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: The `moveTokenDelegates` function incorrectly compares the token owner to the delegatee address (`srcRep`) when deciding which tokens to keep in a checkpoint. This causes all tokens delegated by other users to be dropped from the delegatee's checkpoint whenever a token transfer occurs.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `VotingDelegationLib.sol`, the condition `ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep` is used to retain tokens in `srcRepNew`. However, `srcRep` is the delegatee, while `ownerOfFn(tId)` is the token owner. For delegated tokens, the owner is not the delegatee, so the condition evaluates to false, and the delegated tokens are incorrectly discarded from the checkpoint.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect equality check: ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep instead of tId != _tokenId

preconditions: User A delegates a token to User B
A token transfer or delegation change triggers moveTokenDelegates for User B

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Tokens owned by delegators are dropped from the delegatee's checkpoint
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing correct token ID exclusion logic

analysis_questions: Does any token transfer completely wipe out a delegate's voting power?
Can an attacker maliciously transfer a token to trigger this and zero out a target's votes?
validation_requirements: Test delegation from User A to User B
Trigger moveTokenDelegates and check User B's voting power

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker observes a delegate with massive voting power. Attacker delegates a tiny token to the delegate, then transfers it away. The transfer triggers `moveTokenDelegates`, which drops all other users' delegated tokens from the delegate's checkpoint, zeroing their voting power.
prerequisites: Ability to delegate and transfer tokens

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete breakdown of governance and voting mechanics
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or users to manually re-delegate

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for multi-user delegation and subsequent transfers

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time before a governance snapshot
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily observable on-chain via sudden drop in voting power

threats.title: Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked for AVM-Delegated Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe rewards for tokens delegated to the Automated Voting Manager (AVM) are sent to the `AutoVotingEscrow` contract instead of the original user, permanently locking the funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `Bribes.sol`, `getReward` checks if `_owner == avm` to route rewards to the original owner. However, `avm` is the manager address, while `ownerOf(tokenId)` returns the child `AutoVotingEscrow` contract address. The condition is always false, and rewards are sent to the child contract, which lacks any ERC20 withdrawal functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect address comparison for AVM routing

preconditions: User enables auto-voting via AVM
Bribe rewards accrue to the user's token
GaugeManager calls getReward

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Comparing owner against manager instead of child contract
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Rewards sent to a contract with no withdrawal mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No ERC20 recovery function in AutoVotingEscrow

analysis_questions: Does ownerOf return the child AutoVotingEscrow address?
Is there any way to extract ERC20s from AutoVotingEscrow?
validation_requirements: Trace the getReward execution path for an AVM token
Verify the destination address of the safeTransfer call

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user delegates their veNFT to the AVM. At the end of the epoch, the GaugeManager distributes bribes and calls `getReward`. The Bribe contract sends the user's ERC20 rewards to the `AutoVotingEscrow` contract. The user permanently loses their bribe yield.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 100% loss of bribe rewards for all AVM users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Impossible without upgrading the AutoVotingEscrow contract (which is non-upgradeable)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing end-to-end tests for AVM bribe claiming

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via ERC20 balances accumulating in AutoVotingEscrow contracts

threats.title: RewardsDistributor claim_many Permanently Locks AVM Rebase Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The `claim_many` function in `RewardsDistributor` lacks the AVM routing logic present in `claim()`. For expired AVM locks, it sends rebase rewards directly to the `AutoVotingEscrow` contract, permanently locking them.
threats.investigation_rationale: While `claim()` explicitly checks if the token is in the AVM and routes rewards to `avm.getOriginalOwner()`, `claim_many()` omits this check. It calls `IERC20(token).transfer(_nftOwner, amount)` where `_nftOwner` is the `AutoVotingEscrow` contract. Anyone can call `claim_many` on behalf of others.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing AVM routing logic in batch function

preconditions: Token is delegated to AVM
Token lock has expired
Rebase rewards are available

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Inconsistent logic between single and batch functions

analysis_questions: Can anyone call claim_many for arbitrary token IDs?
Does claim_many bypass the AVM original owner resolution?
validation_requirements: Test claim_many with an expired AVM token
Verify rewards are trapped in the AutoVotingEscrow contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker observes an AVM user with an expired lock and pending rebase rewards. The attacker calls `claim_many` with the user's token ID. The rewards are sent to the `AutoVotingEscrow` contract, permanently depriving the user of their yield.
prerequisites: Target user has expired AVM lock

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of rebase rewards for affected users

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for claim_many with AVM tokens

threats.title: Unbounded Loop in Bribe earned() Causes Permanent DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The `earned` function in `Bribes.sol` iterates over every epoch since the user last claimed. With an epoch duration (`WEEK`) of 1800 seconds (30 minutes), epochs accumulate rapidly, causing the loop to exceed the block gas limit if a user doesn't claim for a few weeks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop iterates `numEpochs` times, where `numEpochs = (current_epoch - lastEarn) / 1800`. One month of inactivity results in 1440 iterations. Each iteration performs multiple storage reads (`checkpoints`, `supplyCheckpoints`). This will quickly exceed the Avalanche block gas limit, permanently locking the user's rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded loop over short time intervals

preconditions: User has staked in Bribe contract
User does not claim rewards for several weeks

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded iteration based on timestamp difference
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Gas limit exceeded due to 30-minute epochs
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum iteration cap or pagination

analysis_questions: How much gas does one iteration of the earned loop consume?
At what point does the loop exceed the 8M/15M gas limit?
validation_requirements: Simulate a 30-day gap between claims
Measure gas consumption of getReward

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user stakes their veNFT and goes on vacation for a month. When they return, they attempt to claim their bribe rewards. The transaction reverts with 'Out of Gas' because the loop tries to process 1440 epochs in a single transaction. The rewards are permanently stuck.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of bribe rewards for inactive users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who do not claim frequently

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Calculate exact gas per iteration to find the breaking point
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via out-of-gas reverts on getReward

threats.title: Merging into SMNFT Burns Tokens Without Decrementing Supply, Causing Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: When a normal lock is merged into an SMNFT, the underlying tokens are burned from the VotingEscrow contract, but the global `supply` variable is not decremented. This causes the contract to become insolvent for normal withdrawals.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `VotingEscrow.merge`, if the target is an SMNFT and the source is not, `_black.burn(value0)` is called to permanently burn the tokens. However, `supply` is not updated. Normal withdrawals rely on the contract having enough balance to cover `supply - smNFTBalance`. Burning tokens without tracking the reduction in supply means the contract will eventually lack the balance to honor normal withdrawals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Token burn without corresponding state variable update

preconditions: User merges a normal lock into an SMNFT
Other users have normal locks pending withdrawal

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Contract balance drops below required reserves

analysis_questions: Does the burn reduce the contract's actual token balance?
Does the failure to decrement supply lead to withdrawal failures?
validation_requirements: Create normal locks for User A and User B
User A merges into SMNFT
User B attempts to withdraw, verify if it reverts due to insufficient balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker creates a large normal lock and an SMNFT. They merge the normal lock into the SMNFT, burning a massive amount of tokens from the VotingEscrow contract. When legitimate users try to withdraw their expired normal locks, the `transfer` fails because the contract's token balance is insufficient.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of funds for normal lock holders due to contract insolvency
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires team to manually send tokens to the contract to restore solvency

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing solvency tests after SMNFT merges

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via contract balance vs supply mismatch

threats.title: Unrestricted SMNFT Creation Allows Permanent Voting Power Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `create_lock` function allows any user to set `isSMNFT = true`. SMNFTs grant a permanent 10% voting power bonus and never decay. This allows anyone to burn tokens for permanent, outsized governance control.
threats.investigation_rationale: The documentation states SMNFTs are designed to 'permanently burn team tokens', implying they should be restricted. However, there is no access control on `isSMNFT` in `create_lock` or `create_lock_for`. An attacker can burn tokens to acquire permanent, non-decaying voting power with a bonus, permanently diluting all other users' voting power and rebase rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing access control on privileged parameter

preconditions: Attacker has BLACK tokens

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unrestricted access to special lock type
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can create permanent, boosted locks
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing role check for isSMNFT == true

analysis_questions: Is SMNFT creation intended to be public?
Does the permanent voting power disrupt gauge emissions?
validation_requirements: Verify any address can call create_lock with isSMNFT=true
Calculate the economic impact of permanent voting power

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker acquires BLACK tokens and calls `create_lock` with `isSMNFT = true`. The tokens are burned, and the attacker receives a permanent veNFT with a 10% voting bonus. They use this permanent voting power to continuously direct gauge emissions to their own pools, extracting outsized yield forever.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent dilution of legitimate voters and misdirection of emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Impossible to reverse as SMNFTs cannot be withdrawn or decayed

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing access control tests for SMNFT creation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via SMNFT creation events from non-team addresses

threats.title: PermissionsRegistry removeRole Corrupts User Role Mappings
threats.hypothesis_description: The `removeRole` function in `PermissionsRegistry` corrupts the `_addressToRoles` mapping by replacing the removed role with the last element of the global `_roles` array, instead of the last element of the user's specific role array.
threats.investigation_rationale: When removing a role from all assigned users, the code does `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][k] = _roles[_roles.length -1];`. It should be `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][k] = _addressToRoles[rta[i]][_addressToRoles[rta[i]].length - 1];`. This assigns a random global role to the user, corrupting their role list and potentially granting them unintended permissions if off-chain systems rely on this view.
preconditions: Admin calls removeRole to delete a role globally

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Assigns global role to specific user

analysis_questions: Does the corruption grant actual permissions via hasRole?
Do any off-chain or on-chain systems rely on addressToRole?
validation_requirements: Call removeRole and inspect addressToRole output for affected users

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The admin decides to deprecate the 'GAUGE_ADMIN' role and calls `removeRole`. The function iterates through all former gauge admins and replaces their 'GAUGE_ADMIN' entry with 'EPOCH_MANAGER' (the last global role). These users now appear as Epoch Managers in the `addressToRole` view, corrupting protocol state.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but breaks role tracking
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires manual state correction via upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for global role removal and state inspection

threats.title: VoterV3 _vote Leaves Token Permanently Locked if Gauges are Dead
threats.hypothesis_description: If a user calls `poke` or `vote` and all their selected gauges are dead, their vote weights are cleared, but the `voted` flag in VotingEscrow remains true. The user cannot transfer or withdraw their token until the next epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `VoterV3._vote`, `_reset` is called first, which clears `votes` and `poolVote`. If all gauges are dead, `_usedWeight` is 0, so `IVotingEscrow(_ve).voting(_tokenId)` is skipped. However, `_reset` does not call `abstain()`. The token's `voted` flag remains true in VE, preventing transfers or withdrawals, and the user cannot call `reset()` until the next epoch due to the `onlyNewEpoch` modifier.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State flag not cleared when condition fails

preconditions: User has previously voted
All voted gauges are killed by governance
User calls poke()

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Token remains marked as voted despite having 0 weight

analysis_questions: Does _reset clear the voted flag?
Can the user call reset() in the same epoch?
validation_requirements: Vote for a gauge, kill the gauge, call poke
Attempt to transfer the veNFT

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user votes for Gauge A. Governance kills Gauge A. The user calls `poke()` to update their lock. Their vote weight is cleared, but the `voted` flag stays true. The user wants to withdraw their expired lock, but `withdraw` reverts because `voted` is true. They must wait until the next epoch to call `reset()`.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary denial of service for withdrawals and transfers

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for poke() behavior with dead gauges

threats.title: Unbounded Loop in AutoVotingEscrowManager getTotalVotingPower
threats.hypothesis_description: The `getTotalVotingPower` function iterates over all AVM instances, and each instance iterates over all its locks (up to 1024). This nested loop will quickly exceed the block gas limit, causing the function to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AutoVotingEscrowManager.sol`, `getTotalVotingPower` loops over `avms.length`. Inside `AutoVotingEscrow.sol`, `getTotalVotingPower` loops over `locks.length` (max 1024) and calls `balanceOfNFT`, which performs a binary search. With just 2-3 full AVMs, the gas required will exceed the Avalanche block gas limit (8M-15M).

proof_of_signal.pattern: Nested unbounded loops with heavy internal calls

preconditions: Multiple AVM instances are created and filled with locks

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pagination or cached total tracking

analysis_questions: How much gas does one balanceOfNFT call consume?
Is getTotalVotingPower used by any critical on-chain logic?
validation_requirements: Fill 2 AVMs with 1024 locks each
Call getTotalVotingPower and measure gas

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker creates many small locks and enables auto-voting, filling up several AVM instances. Any external contract or UI attempting to read `getTotalVotingPower` receives an Out of Gas revert, breaking integrations.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service for external integrations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to implement cached tracking

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing gas profiling for full AVMs

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Calculate max safe locks before OOG

threats.title: VotingEscrow merge Allows Expired Locks to Convert to SMNFT
threats.hypothesis_description: The `merge` function checks that the target lock is active, but fails to check if the source lock is active. This allows users to merge expired locks into SMNFTs, gaining permanent bonus voting power instead of withdrawing.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `VotingEscrow.merge`, the require statement `require(_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent, "EXP||PERM");` only validates the target (`_to`). There is no validation that `_locked0.end > block.timestamp`. A user with an expired lock can merge it into an SMNFT, burning the tokens but receiving the 10% SMNFT voting bonus permanently.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing state validation on source object

preconditions: User has an expired normal lock
User has an active SMNFT lock

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Expired locks can be revived as permanent boosted locks

analysis_questions: Can an expired lock be merged into an SMNFT?
Does the user receive the SMNFT bonus for the expired tokens?
validation_requirements: Create a lock and let it expire
Merge it into an SMNFT
Verify the SMNFT balance increases with the bonus

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user's 4-year lock expires. Instead of withdrawing the tokens, they merge the expired lock into an SMNFT. The protocol burns the tokens and grants the user permanent voting power equal to the expired amount + 10% bonus. The user extracts value from tokens that were supposed to exit the voting system.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unintended inflation of permanent voting power

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for merging expired locks

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via merge events involving expired source tokens

threats.title: Signature Replay in delegateBySig Due to Non-Incrementing Nonce
threats.hypothesis_description: The `delegate` function does not increment the user's nonce. If a user signs a delegation message and later changes their delegation directly via `delegate()`, the old signature remains valid and can be replayed to override the new delegation.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `VotingEscrow.sol`, `delegateBySig` verifies `nonce == nonces[signatory]++`. However, calling `delegate()` directly does not increment `nonces[msg.sender]`. An attacker holding a previously signed `delegateBySig` message can submit it at any time to revert the user's delegation back to the attacker, overriding any recent direct delegations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State variable (nonce) not updated during equivalent state change

preconditions: User signs a delegateBySig message
User later calls delegate() directly

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Old signatures remain valid after state changes

analysis_questions: Does delegate() increment the nonce?
Can a signed message be submitted after a direct delegation?
validation_requirements: Sign delegation to User A
Call delegate(User B) directly
Submit signature for User A, verify delegation changes to User A

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Alice signs a delegation to Bob. Later, Alice changes her mind and calls `delegate(Charlie)` directly. Bob observes this and submits Alice's original signature. Because Alice's nonce didn't increment, the signature is accepted, and Alice's voting power is forcibly redirected back to Bob.
prerequisites: Attacker possesses a signed delegation message

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of governance control for affected users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: User must call delegate() again and increment nonce manually via dummy signature

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests mixing direct delegation and signature delegation

threats.title: AutoVotingEscrow lockOwner Returns Incorrect Owner for Unmapped Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The `lockOwner` function in `AutoVotingEscrow` returns the owner of the first lock in the array for any token ID that has not been deposited, due to the default mapping value of 0.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AutoVotingEscrow.sol`, `tokenIdIndex[_tokenId]` defaults to 0 for unmapped tokens. If the `locks` array is not empty, `lockOwner` returns `locks[0].owner` instead of reverting or returning `address(0)`. This could allow an attacker to spoof ownership of a token if an external contract relies on `lockOwner` without verifying `tokenIdToAVMId`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Default mapping value used as array index without validation

preconditions: AVM instance has at least one lock
External contract queries lockOwner for a non-existent token

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unvalidated array access using default mapping value
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Returns valid owner for invalid token

analysis_questions: Do any contracts rely on lockOwner for authorization?
Does this affect getOriginalOwner in the manager?
validation_requirements: Query lockOwner for a random token ID
Verify it returns locks[0].owner

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An external integration queries `getOriginalOwner` or `lockOwner` to verify if a user owns a specific token in the AVM. The attacker passes a token ID they don't own, but the function returns the address of the first user in the AVM. If the attacker is that first user, they successfully spoof ownership of the arbitrary token.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential authorization bypass in external integrations

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for querying unmapped tokens in AVM

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect on-chain as it's a view function issue
